clusters.title: Global study estimates over 250,000 meningitis deaths in 2023, with young children bearing a heavy toll
clusters.summary: In 2023, 259,000 people died from meningitis and 2.5 million people were infected with the disease globally, suggests a study published in The Lancet Neurology. Although death and infection rates have declined significantly since 1990, progress is insufficient to meet the WHO targets of a 50% reduction in infections and 70% reduction in deaths by 2030.

items.title: Global study estimates over 250,000 meningitis deaths in 2023, with young children bearing a heavy toll
items.description: In 2023, 259,000 people died from meningitis and 2.5 million people were infected with the disease globally, suggests a study published in The Lancet Neurology. Although death and infection rates have declined significantly since 1990, progress is insufficient to meet the WHO targets of a 50% reduction in infections and 70% reduction in deaths by 2030.

clusters.title: Excessive smartphone habits tied to emotional dysregulation in the brain
clusters.summary: A newly released neuroimaging study reveals that young adults who heavily overuse smartphones show altered functional connectivity in the amygdala. These specific neural differences correlate to everyday difficulties in managing negative emotions.

items.title: Excessive smartphone habits tied to emotional dysregulation in the brain
items.description: A newly released neuroimaging study reveals that young adults who heavily overuse smartphones show altered functional connectivity in the amygdala. These specific neural differences correlate to everyday difficulties in managing negative emotions.

clusters.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
clusters.summary: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8.

items.title: One in three people carry this brain parasite but the body has a kill switch
items.description: A parasite that may already be hiding in your brain has a shocking survival trick: it can infect the very immune cells sent to destroy it. Yet most people never get sick, and new research from UVA Health reveals why. Scientists discovered that when Toxoplasma gondii invades CD8+ T cells — key defenders of the immune system — those cells can trigger a self-destruct mechanism powered by an enzyme called caspase-8. By sacrificing themselves, the infected cells also wipe out the parasite inside them.

clusters.title: Cutting sweet foods doesn’t reduce cravings or improve health
clusters.summary: A new study suggests that eating less sweet food doesn’t make people crave it any less—or improve their health markers. Participants who increased or decreased sweetness in their diets showed no changes in preferences, weight, or disease risk. Many even returned to their old eating habits over time.

items.title: Cutting sweet foods doesn’t reduce cravings or improve health
items.description: A new study suggests that eating less sweet food doesn’t make people crave it any less—or improve their health markers. Participants who increased or decreased sweetness in their diets showed no changes in preferences, weight, or disease risk. Many even returned to their old eating habits over time. Researchers say it may be time to rethink guidelines that focus on cutting sweetness instead of reducing sugar and calories.

clusters.title: Study reveals how live bacterial therapy reshapes the vaginal microbiome and identifies predictors of treatment success
clusters.summary: A new study from the Kwon Lab at the Ragon Institute, published in Cell Host & Microbe, provides the most detailed picture yet of how a promising bacterial therapy works to prevent recurrent bacterial vaginosis (BV) and why it works better for some women than others.

items.title: Study reveals how live bacterial therapy reshapes the vaginal microbiome and identifies predictors of treatment success
items.description: A new study from the Kwon Lab at the Ragon Institute, published in Cell Host & Microbe, provides the most detailed picture yet of how a promising bacterial therapy works to prevent recurrent bacterial vaginosis (BV) and why it works better for some women than others.

clusters.title: This dangerous combo in your body could raise death risk by 83%
clusters.summary: Having both excess belly fat and low muscle mass isn’t just unhealthy—it’s potentially deadly, raising the risk of death by 83%. This condition, called sarcopenic obesity, creates a vicious cycle where fat accelerates muscle breakdown and inflammation. Researchers found it can be identified using simple measurements, not costly medical tests.

items.title: This dangerous combo in your body could raise death risk by 83%
items.description: Having both excess belly fat and low muscle mass isn’t just unhealthy—it’s potentially deadly, raising the risk of death by 83%. This condition, called sarcopenic obesity, creates a vicious cycle where fat accelerates muscle breakdown and inflammation. Researchers found it can be identified using simple measurements, not costly medical tests. That means earlier detection—and a real chance to intervene before serious decline sets in.

clusters.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
clusters.summary: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm.

items.title: Why tipping keeps rising and may not improve service
items.description: Why do we tip—even when we know we’ll never see the server again? New research suggests it’s not just about rewarding good service, but about social pressure. Some people tip out of genuine appreciation, while others simply follow the norm. But here’s the twist: those who truly value great service tend to tip more than average, and everyone else adjusts upward to match them.

clusters.title: Depression is linked to a genuine pessimistic bias rather than a realistic view of the world
clusters.summary: Challenging the idea of depressive realism, new research shows that depression causes an active pessimistic bias. People with high depressive symptoms consistently underestimate the likelihood of positive events actually occurring in their everyday lives.

items.title: Depression is linked to a genuine pessimistic bias rather than a realistic view of the world
items.description: Challenging the idea of depressive realism, new research shows that depression causes an active pessimistic bias. People with high depressive symptoms consistently underestimate the likelihood of positive events actually occurring in their everyday lives.

clusters.title: Scientists discover a brain signal that may trigger autism’s domino effect
clusters.summary: Researchers have uncovered a surprising molecular chain reaction in the brain that may play a role in some forms of autism. The study suggests that nitric oxide, a tiny signaling molecule normally involved in fine-tuning communication between brain cells, can sometimes trigger a cascade of changes inside neurons. When nitric oxide activity rises, it can alter a protective protein called TSC2, weakening an important cellular brake and allowing the mTOR pathway, which controls growth and protein production, to become overactive.

items.title: Scientists discover a brain signal that may trigger autism’s domino effect
items.description: Researchers have uncovered a surprising molecular chain reaction in the brain that may play a role in some forms of autism. The study suggests that nitric oxide, a tiny signaling molecule normally involved in fine-tuning communication between brain cells, can sometimes trigger a cascade of changes inside neurons. When nitric oxide activity rises, it can alter a protective protein called TSC2, weakening an important cellular brake and allowing the mTOR pathway, which controls growth and protein production, to become overactive.

clusters.title: Scientists link childhood stress to lifelong digestive issues
clusters.summary: Early life stress may set the stage for long-term digestive problems by disrupting the gut-brain connection. Studies in both mice and thousands of children found links to symptoms like pain, constipation, and IBS. Scientists discovered that different biological pathways control different gut issues, hinting at more personalized treatments in the future.

items.title: Scientists link childhood stress to lifelong digestive issues
items.description: Early life stress may set the stage for long-term digestive problems by disrupting the gut-brain connection. Studies in both mice and thousands of children found links to symptoms like pain, constipation, and IBS. Scientists discovered that different biological pathways control different gut issues, hinting at more personalized treatments in the future. The research also highlights how a child’s early environment can have lasting physical effects—not just emotional ones.

clusters.title: Women experience greater jealousy when their romantic rivals have highly feminine faces
clusters.summary: A new study reveals that women experience greater romantic jealousy when a potential rival has highly feminine facial features. While this effect occurs in both heterosexual and lesbian women, it is significantly stronger among straight women.

items.title: Women experience greater jealousy when their romantic rivals have highly feminine faces
items.description: A new study reveals that women experience greater romantic jealousy when a potential rival has highly feminine facial features. While this effect occurs in both heterosexual and lesbian women, it is significantly stronger among straight women.

clusters.title: Noise reduction measures beneficial for patients with Meniere Disease
clusters.summary: For patients with Meniere disease, implementing noise reduction measures during noise-sensitive periods is associated with improved vertigo symptoms, reduced tinnitus handicap, and enhanced hearing function, according to a study published in the January-February issue of Noise & Health.

items.title: Noise reduction measures beneficial for patients with Meniere Disease
items.description: For patients with Meniere disease, implementing noise reduction measures during noise-sensitive periods is associated with improved vertigo symptoms, reduced tinnitus handicap, and enhanced hearing function, according to a study published in the January-February issue of Noise & Health.

clusters.title: Scientists say NAD+ could slow aging and fight Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s
clusters.summary: A global team of leading scientists is zeroing in on a tiny but powerful molecule that could reshape how we age. Known as NAD⁺, it plays a crucial role in keeping our cells energized, repairing DNA, and maintaining overall health—but its levels steadily decline over time, potentially fueling diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s. Researchers are now exploring ways to boost NAD⁺ using compounds like NR and NMN, with early studies hinting at improvements in memory, metabolism, and physical function.

items.title: Scientists say NAD+ could slow aging and fight Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s
items.description: A global team of leading scientists is zeroing in on a tiny but powerful molecule that could reshape how we age. Known as NAD⁺, it plays a crucial role in keeping our cells energized, repairing DNA, and maintaining overall health—but its levels steadily decline over time, potentially fueling diseases like Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s. Researchers are now exploring ways to boost NAD⁺ using compounds like NR and NMN, with early studies hinting at improvements in memory, metabolism, and physical function.

clusters.title: Trying harder on an intelligence test does not actually improve your score
clusters.summary: Financial incentives can motivate people to try harder on cognitive tests, but this increased motivation does not improve their actual scores. A recent study reveals that intelligence metrics accurately reflect mental ability rather than simply measuring willingness to work.

items.title: Trying harder on an intelligence test does not actually improve your score
items.description: Financial incentives can motivate people to try harder on cognitive tests, but this increased motivation does not improve their actual scores. A recent study reveals that intelligence metrics accurately reflect mental ability rather than simply measuring willingness to work.

clusters.title: How to contain avian flu H5N1 if human-to-human spread begins
clusters.summary: At this point, avian flu H5N1 is thought to have very limited ability to transmit between humans, but a recent case in British Columbia with an unknown source of transmission has piqued the curiosity and concern of scientists, including York University Professor Seyed Moghadas. Did this lone case come about through transmission from an animal or another person, and if it was via human transmission, what methods would control its spread in the human population?

items.title: How to contain avian flu H5N1 if human-to-human spread begins
items.description: At this point, avian flu H5N1 is thought to have very limited ability to transmit between humans, but a recent case in British Columbia with an unknown source of transmission has piqued the curiosity and concern of scientists, including York University Professor Seyed Moghadas. Did this lone case come about through transmission from an animal or another person, and if it was via human transmission, what methods would control its spread in the human population?

clusters.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
clusters.summary: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.

items.title: Scientists find immune cell linked to long COVID fatigue and symptoms
items.description: Long COVID remains a frustrating medical mystery, affecting up to 1 in 10 people long after the initial infection fades. Now, scientists have uncovered a crucial clue hidden deep within the immune system. By analyzing individual immune cells, they identified a distinct molecular state in key white blood cells—especially common in patients who initially had mild or moderate COVID.

clusters.title: Doctors implant dopamine-producing stem cells in Parkinson’s patients
clusters.summary: A groundbreaking clinical trial is testing whether specially engineered stem cells can help the brain restore its own dopamine production in people with Parkinson’s disease. Because the condition is driven by the gradual loss of dopamine-producing cells—leading to tremors, stiffness, and slowed movement—researchers are implanting lab-grown cells directly into the brain’s movement center to replace what’s been lost.

items.title: Doctors implant dopamine-producing stem cells in Parkinson’s patients
items.description: A groundbreaking clinical trial is testing whether specially engineered stem cells can help the brain restore its own dopamine production in people with Parkinson’s disease. Because the condition is driven by the gradual loss of dopamine-producing cells—leading to tremors, stiffness, and slowed movement—researchers are implanting lab-grown cells directly into the brain’s movement center to replace what’s been lost.

clusters.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
clusters.summary: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center.

items.title: This ancient sea creature may already have had a brain
items.description: Advanced 3D reconstructions of the comb jelly’s aboral organ reveal a sensory system far more complex than scientists expected. The organ contains a wide variety of specialized cells and is closely linked to the animal’s nerve network, allowing it to coordinate behavior and orientation in the water. Researchers say it may function as a primitive brain-like center. The discovery suggests that centralized nervous systems might have evolved independently in different animal lineages.

clusters.title: Insulin pills may soon replace daily injections
clusters.summary: For over a century, scientists have chased the dream of insulin pills, but the digestive system kept destroying the drug before it could work—forcing millions of patients to rely on daily injections. Now, researchers at Kumamoto University have developed a clever workaround using a tiny peptide that helps insulin slip through the intestinal wall.

clusters.title: Audio tapes reveal mass rule-breaking in Milgram’s obedience experiments
clusters.summary: A fresh analysis of audio recordings from Stanley Milgram's infamous shock experiments reveals that his most obedient volunteers routinely broke the rules of the supposed scientific study.

items.title: Audio tapes reveal mass rule-breaking in Milgram’s obedience experiments
